title: T28.5 migration-equivalence gate: file ADR waiver or write seed-policy integration tests
description:
## Goal

Discharge the T28.5 spec obligation (PR #106 `07-migration-seeds.md` MUST requirement) that was bypassed without ADR documentation. The compiler enforces absence of `StaticAccessControl` (the type was deleted), but `internal/access/migration_equivalence_test.go` was never created. The spec required a migration-equivalence test as the T28.5 prerequisite; T29 ran without it.

## Design reference

- Originally filed as `holomush-gqjb.10`. Promoted 2026-05-15. Flagged by 2026-04-26 audit and 2026-05-15 audit; still unresolved.
- Audit report: `.claude/agent-memory/bead-auditor/reports/2026-05-15-1106-gqjb-children.md`.
- Spec: `07-migration-seeds.md` T28.5 (the bypassed gate).

## Plan reference

Two options for resolving the spec compliance gap:

- **Option A: ADR waiver** — file `docs/adr/<bd-id>-static-access-control-compile-enforcement.md` citing that compiler enforcement of `StaticAccessControl` absence supersedes the migration-equivalence test requirement. Reference the PR #106 commit that deleted the type.
- **Option B: Write the equivalent tests** — add seed-policy integration tests (`internal/access/policy/seed_smoke_test.go` already covers some) that exercise the same coverage the migration-equivalence test would have. Document the mapping.

Pick one.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- ADR file exists in `docs/adr/` (Option A), OR
- Seed-policy integration tests cover the player-role / location-based read paths that T28.5 would have validated (Option B).

## Verification steps

1. ADR present and referenced from PR #106's audit trail (Option A).
2. `task test:int` shows the new seed-policy tests passing (Option B).
3. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `docs/adr/` (Option A)
- `internal/access/policy/seed_smoke_test.go` or `test/integration/access/` (Option B)

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Other deferred spec items from PR #106 (file separately if any).

title: CI static-analysis guard to prevent AccessControl.Check reintroduction
description:
## Goal

Add a CI/lint guard that fails the build if `AccessControl.Check` (the removed legacy interface) is re-introduced in production code. The compiler enforces absence today (the type was deleted in PR #106), but there's no guard against accidentally re-importing or re-implementing it. Spec §10 (PR #106) required this guard; the deferral was never documented via ADR.

Two related findings consolidated:
- `holomush-hqi6.25` — golangci `forbidigo` rule for `AccessControl.Check`
- `holomush-gqjb.13` — CI grep step for the same

## Design reference

- Originally filed as `holomush-hqi6.25` + `holomush-gqjb.13`. Consolidated 2026-05-15.
- Spec: PR #106 §10 required CI guard against `AccessControl.Check` reintroduction.
- Audit reports: `.claude/agent-memory/bead-auditor/reports/2026-05-15-1105-hqi6-pr106-review.md` and `2026-05-15-1106-gqjb-children.md`.

## Plan reference

Two reasonable options:

- **Option A: `golangci-lint forbidigo`** — add a forbidigo entry in `.golangci.yaml` for `AccessControl\.Check` pattern. Runs as part of `task lint`. Clean tooling but requires the codebase to still have golangci-lint scanning the cited packages.
- **Option B: CI grep step** — one-line check in `.github/workflows/ci.yaml`: `rg "AccessControl\.Check" --type go internal/ && exit 1`. Lower-tech but always runs.

Pick one and document the choice.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- Re-introducing `AccessControl.Check` in any production Go file causes `task lint` (or CI) to fail.
- A unit-test or integration-test stub that legitimately needs the symbol can be allowlisted (only if there's a real need — current state is zero references).

## Verification steps

1. `task lint` passes today (no current uses).
2. Temporarily re-add `AccessControl.Check(ctx, ...)` to a Go file — lint/CI fails.
3. Remove the temporary line — lint/CI passes.

## Files touched

- `.golangci.yaml` (Option A)
- OR `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` (Option B)

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Adding similar guards for other removed symbols (file separately if desired).

title: KV hostfunc: log decision reason on denial (currently silently dropped)
description:
## Goal

Fix `internal/plugin/hostfunc/functions.go:310-311` — the `!decision.IsAllowed()` denial branch returns `"access denied"` to Lua with no server-side log. KV denial is undiagnosable in production. Plugin authors get a generic message and operators have no record of which policy denied which subject/action.

2. Consider `slog.Info` if denials should be more visible operationally.

## TDD acceptance criteria

title: Audit logger: differentiate ModeMinimal from ModeDenialsOnly (identical implementations)
description:
## Goal

Fix `internal/audit/logger.go` — `ModeMinimal` and `ModeDenialsOnly` have identical implementations. They were intended to differ (Minimal = bare-minimum audit, DenialsOnly = log only deny outcomes), but in current code they behave the same.

**Status note:** Originally filed as `holomush-wfza.62`. Sub-fix `holomush-wfza.69` was created and CLOSED but the code change NEVER LANDED — bead-auditor caught this on 2026-05-15 as a FALSE-FIX pattern.

## Design reference

- Originally filed as `holomush-wfza.62` (with fix-bead `wfza.69`). Promoted 2026-05-15.
- Audit report: `.claude/agent-memory/bead-auditor/reports/2026-05-15-supplement-wfza.md`.

## Plan reference

Decide the actual semantic distinction:

- **Option A:** Define each mode's exact behavior in spec (e.g., Minimal logs all + skips fields; DenialsOnly logs deny+effect-system-bypass only). Implement.
- **Option B:** If one mode is redundant, delete the redundant one and update callers.

Then land the actual code change (verify in current code before closing this bead).

## TDD acceptance criteria

- Unit test asserts `ModeMinimal` and `ModeDenialsOnly` produce different log output for the same input.
- Existing tests still pass.

## Verification steps

1. `task test -- ./internal/audit/...` green.
2. Diff the two mode codepaths — they differ.
3. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/audit/logger.go` (mode implementations)
- `internal/audit/logger_test.go` (mode-differentiation tests)

## Out of scope

- Audit DLQ (`holomush-1tvn.17`).

title: ABAC Phase 7.7 cleanup + design followups
description:
## Goal

Address 3 cleanup / design-question items from the 2026-05-15 audit of PR #114 (ABAC Phase 7.7). Each is small but each requires a deliberate decision rather than a mechanical fix.

## Design reference

- Originally filed as 3 separate beads under `holomush-fxa9`: `fxa9.19`, `fxa9.20`, `fxa9.39`.
- Audit report: `.claude/agent-memory/bead-auditor/reports/2026-05-15-1019-fxa9-pr114.md`.

## Plan reference

### 1. `ScanPoliciesForAttributes` regex compile error handling (`fxa9.19`)

- Code: `schema_evolution.go:83-87` — on regex compile error, code logs `slog.Warn` then `continue`s. Pattern is skipped without error propagation to caller.
- Decision needed: is silently skipping a compile error acceptable (caller still gets partial results), or should it return an error?
- Likely outcome: return an error from `ScanPoliciesForAttributes`; caller decides whether to fail-closed or use partial results.

### 2. Duplicate `validateEntityRef` (`fxa9.20`)

- Code: `engine.go` has input validation at Evaluate Step 4 (`validateEntityRef`); `resolver.go:285-292` has `validateEntityRef`. The two implementations differ slightly (one uses `TrimSpace`, the other does not).
- Decision needed: pick one canonical implementation; have both call sites use it. Or document why they intentionally differ.
- Likely outcome: lift to a shared helper in `internal/access/policy/types/` or `internal/access/`.

### 3. Schema evolution review flag persistence (`fxa9.39`)

- Code: `schema_evolution.go:188-196` — `UpdateNamespace` only `slog.Warn`s on referenced attributes; doesn't return a `PolicyReference` slice or persist a flag.
- Decision needed: decide whether to (a) return references to caller, (b) persist a `needs_review` flag (overlaps with `fxa9.45` in production-bug bead), or (c) document the warning-only behavior as intentional.
- Likely outcome: (b), but coordinate with the production-bug bead's resolution of `fxa9.45`.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- Each decision recorded in a `bd note` or this bead's description before implementation.
- New tests verify the chosen behavior.

## Verification steps

1. `task test -- ./internal/access/policy/...` green.
2. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/access/policy/schema_evolution.go` (regex error handling, namespace flag)
- `internal/access/policy/engine.go` + `internal/access/policy/resolver.go` (validateEntityRef dedup)
- Corresponding `_test.go` files

## Dependencies

- Coordinates with the production-bug consolidated bead (for `fxa9.39` ↔ `fxa9.45` overlap).

## Out of scope

- Missing features (separate consolidated bead).
- Test-only gaps (separate consolidated bead).

title: AccessPolicyEngine call-site observability gaps (metric labels + span attributes)
description:
## Goal

Fix two related observability gaps in AccessPolicyEngine call-sites that surfaced during the 2026-05-15 audit of PR #88. Both are about distinguishing infrastructure-failure errors from policy-deny outcomes in observability output, so operators can tell at a glance which kind of failure is happening.

## Design reference

- Originally filed as 4 separate beads under `holomush-eulm`: `eulm.1` (canExecuteCommand early-return inflates circuit breaker count + single metric label) + `eulm.15` (fix-task: differentiate metric labels) + `eulm.6` (RateLimitMiddleware bypass error not on span/trace) + `eulm.14` (fix-task: add span attribute).
- Audit report: `.claude/agent-memory/bead-auditor/reports/2026-05-15-1016-eulm-pr88-review.md`.

## Plan reference

Two scoped fixes:

### 1. Metric label split in `internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go`

- Currently: `canExecuteCommand` uses one metric label for both request-build errors and engine errors.
- Fix: introduce two metric names or labels — `command_capability_request_error` (request-build failures, pre-engine) vs `command_capability_engine_error` (engine evaluation failures).
- Also: review whether `canExecuteCommand` should short-circuit on first error (current behavior: inflates the circuit-breaker count by 1 per command) or accumulate and report.

### 2. Span attribute for bypass error in `internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go`

- Currently: `RateLimitMiddleware.Enforce` calls `hasBypass(ctx)` returning `(bool, error)`; on error the code logs and drops the error (fail-closed to rate limiting).
- Fix: even when the error is dropped, attach a `rate_limit.bypass_check_error=true` span attribute so traces show that bypass evaluation failed (vs. genuinely denied).

## TDD acceptance criteria

- New tests assert the metric-label split (two distinct labels seen under matching conditions).
- New tests assert the span attribute is set on bypass-check failure.
- Existing dispatcher / rate-limit tests still pass.

## Verification steps

1. `task test -- ./internal/plugin/hostfunc/... ./internal/command/...` green.
2. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go` (metric label split)
- `internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands_test.go` (new label-coverage tests)
- `internal/command/rate_limit_middleware.go` (span attribute)
- `internal/command/rate_limit_middleware_test.go` (new span attribute test)

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Audit-log failure signaling (separate top-level bead — promoted from eulm.2).
- session-invalid reclassification at call-site (deferred to holomush-wfza.21).

title: Lift focus.Coordinator from internal/grpc/focus/ to internal/focus/
description:
## Goal

Move `focus.Coordinator` out of `internal/grpc/focus/` and into `internal/focus/`. The `internal/plugin/manager.go:26` import shows the coordinator is consumed by the plugin manager — that means it's not a gRPC-layer concern. Either lift it (preferred) or hide the import behind an interface defined inside `internal/plugin/`.

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §7, §8.P2.12
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` Wave 1

## Plan reference

Direct move (mechanical):

1. `git mv internal/grpc/focus/* internal/focus/` (or jj equivalent).
2. Update the package declaration if needed.
3. Update every importer (`rg -l "internal/grpc/focus"`).
4. Verify `internal/grpc/` no longer holds the package.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- All existing focus tests pass under the new path.
- `rg "internal/grpc/focus"` returns zero hits.

## Verification steps

1. `task test -- ./internal/focus/...` green.
2. `task test:int` green (any test exercising focus via plugin path).
3. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/grpc/focus/*.go` → `internal/focus/*.go`
- `internal/plugin/manager.go:26` (import path)
- Every other importer (run `rg -l "internal/grpc/focus"` to enumerate)

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Re-shaping the `Coordinator` API.
- Changing how the plugin manager consumes the coordinator (still passes a value into `ConfigureFocusDeps`).
notes: RE-TRIAGE 2026-05-29: KEEP, REVISIT acceptance criterion. Layering concern persists: internal/grpc/focus/coordinator.go (290 LOC, package focus) still under the grpc gateway package; internal/focus/ does NOT exist; internal/plugin/manager.go:26 imports internal/grpc/focus (the cross-layer dep the bead flags). PR #4314 (holomush-66228, v0.5.0) unified focus-delta logic but did NOT lift the package. The bead's TDD criterion 'rg internal/grpc/focus -> 0 hits' is now too strict — legitimate in-grpc importers remain (server.go, sub_grpc.go, focus_wiring.go, integration tests). Tighten the criterion to PRODUCTION NON-gRPC importers only (the plugin manager is the real violation). P2 correct.

title: Add AuthGuard audit metrics counters (drain_failed, marshal_failed)
description:
## Goal

Wire OpenTelemetry counters for the two unactioned `TODO(metrics):` markers in `internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/emitter.go:216,246`:

- `drain_failed` — audit queue drain failure.
- `marshal_failed` — audit row marshalling failure.

Both are visibility gaps in the security-critical decrypt-audit path. Silent failure of the audit emit is currently bounded by INV-19 failing the read closed, but operators have no signal on *why* it failed (queue full vs marshal error vs DB error).

Note: the related "audit DLQ" half of audit-finding #11 is covered separately by `holomush-1tvn.17` — this bead handles ONLY the AuthGuard emitter metrics.

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §5.3, §8.P2.11
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` §D6 (split from 1tvn.17), Wave 1
- TODOs: `internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/emitter.go:216,246`

## Plan reference

Direct fix:

1. Define the two counters in whatever observability package `internal/eventbus/authguard/` uses.
2. Increment at the two TODO sites.
3. Add a unit test that triggers each failure mode and asserts the counter incremented.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- Unit test: drain-failure path increments `drain_failed`.
- Unit test: marshal-failure path increments `marshal_failed`.
- `task test -- ./internal/eventbus/authguard/...` green.

## Verification steps

1. `task test -- ./internal/eventbus/authguard/...` green.
2. `rg "TODO\\(metrics\\)" internal/eventbus/authguard/` returns zero hits (or only unrelated TODOs).
3. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/emitter.go` (counter increments)
- `internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/metrics.go` or wherever counters are declared (new or extended)
- Test file under `internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/`

## Dependencies

- None.
- Cross-link: `holomush-1tvn.17` covers the audit DLQ TODO at `internal/eventbus/audit/subsystem.go:59` — separate scope.

## Out of scope

- The audit DLQ itself (`1tvn.17`).
- Re-shaping the AuthGuard audit emit pipeline.
notes: Cross-link per triage §D6: holomush-1tvn.17 (Wire EVENTS_AUDIT_DLQ for audit projection MaxDeliver exhaustion) covers the audit DLQ TODO at internal/eventbus/audit/subsystem.go:59 — that's the OTHER half of audit finding #11 and is NOT in this bead's scope. This bead covers only the AuthGuard audit emitter counters at internal/eventbus/authguard/audit/emitter.go:216,246.

title: Document permitted Lua/binary asymmetries in plugin-runtime-symmetry.md
description:
## Goal

Add a "permitted runtime-specific asymmetries" table to `.claude/rules/plugin-runtime-symmetry.md` so reviewers know what to flag and what to accept. Current rule says "Binary and Lua plugins MUST be treated identically by the host," but the manifest-validation surface (`internal/plugin/manifest.go`) rejects Lua plugins from `audit`, `provides`, `storage: postgres`, and `resource_types` — these are real capability gradients the rule must document explicitly.

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §3, §8.P2.10
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` Wave 1
- Code cites: `internal/plugin/manifest.go:511-514` (audit reject), `:523-527` (provides), `:530-534` (storage), `:543-545` (resource_types)

## Plan reference

Documentation-only update. Read each manifest-rejection site to extract the actual justification, then table them into the rule file.

Suggested table shape:

| Asymmetry | Why permitted | Code cite |
|---|---|---|
| Lua cannot declare `audit` blocks | Binary plugins implement gRPC PluginAuditService; Lua has no service surface. | `internal/plugin/manifest.go:511-514` |
| Lua cannot declare `provides` | Same — Lua has no gRPC server. | `:523-527` |
| Lua cannot use `storage: postgres` | Same. | `:530-534` |
| Lua cannot declare `resource_types` | Same — no service to register types with. | `:543-545` |

## TDD acceptance criteria

- Rule file lists the four permitted asymmetries with code cites.
- Spot-check by reading each cite confirms the description matches reality.

## Verification steps

1. Read `.claude/rules/plugin-runtime-symmetry.md` — table present.
2. Read each cited line — code matches description.
3. `task pr-prep` green (docs-only fast lane).

## Files touched

- `.claude/rules/plugin-runtime-symmetry.md`

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Actually widening Lua to own these capabilities (separate design discussion).
- Changing manifest-validation logic.
notes: Permitted-asymmetry doc PARTIALLY ADVANCED (2026-06-20): added a 'Permitted asymmetry: same capability, different transport' section to .claude/rules/plugin-runtime-symmetry.md documenting the world-read case — Lua reaches world reads via the world.query host capability, binary via the holomush.world.v1.WorldService service, both through the same world.Service ABAC chokepoint; binary goplugin/host.go WorldQuerier()->nil is INTENTIONAL. Also corrected the misleading host.go WorldQuerier/WorldMutator comments. Context: a scene-name-resolution design (holomush-vdy2z) briefly mis-filed a duplicate parity bead (holomush-udfqi, now closed) off the nil-WorldQuerier misread, re-opening retired q42fh. The manifest-validation gradient table (audit/provides/storage/resource_types) this bead originally scoped is still TODO.

title: Reclassify internal/admin/policy/ under auditchain or eventbus/crypto/policy
description:
## Goal

Move `internal/admin/policy/` (2111 LOC — policy-chain verification, chain reduction, emitter, subsystem) out of `admin/` and under a more representative parent. The package's lifecycle ID is `SubsystemCryptoPolicy` (in the crypto family), it depends on `auditchain`, and it is fundamentally a chain-verifier — its placement under `admin/` makes it look like an admin-CLI feature.

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §1.4, §8.P2.9
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` Wave 2
- Direction: "move `internal/admin/policy/` to `internal/auditchain/policy/` OR `internal/eventbus/crypto/policy/`."

## Plan reference

`phase:design` — but the design scope is narrow: **destination selection only**. Once decided, the move itself is mechanical (directory rename + import updates).

Brainstorm decides:

- Destination: `internal/auditchain/policy/` vs `internal/eventbus/crypto/policy/`. Pick based on subsystem-ID family and existing dependencies.
- Whether to leave a thin admin-CLI client at `internal/admin/policy/` that imports from the new location.

Implementation post-brainstorm is one PR.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- After move, all existing tests pass.
- `rg "internal/admin/policy"` returns only the thin admin-CLI client (if kept) or zero hits.

## Verification steps

1. `task test:int` green (covers policy-chain verification).
2. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/admin/policy/*.go` → `internal/<destination>/policy/*.go`
- Every importer (run `rg -l "internal/admin/policy"`)
- Subsystem registration sites

## Dependencies

- None.

## Out of scope

- Re-shaping the policy-chain verifier internals.
- Changing the admin-CLI command surface.

title: Rename PostgresEventStore → SystemInfoStore + switch InitGameID to idgen.New()
description:
## Goal

Two related fixes that share a file:

1. Rename `PostgresEventStore` (`internal/store/postgres.go:33-35`) → `SystemInfoStore` (or fold into `internal/bootstrap/`). The type stores no events post-F7 — only `SystemInfo` and `GameID`. The current name is a fossil.
2. Switch `InitGameID` (`internal/store/postgres.go:108`) from `core.NewULID().String()` to `idgen.New()`. The gameID is an entity primary key (identity), not an event ID (ordering); per `CLAUDE.md` ULID-generation rule it should use the entropy-fresh generator.

Also absorbs `holomush-d0tn.20` (PostgresEventStore.Close error handling) — close `d0tn.20` with reason "superseded by dj95.8" once this bead is filed (per triage §D7).

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §2.1, §6.1, §8.P2.8
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` §D7 (d0tn.20 fold), Wave 1
- Rule: `CLAUDE.md` §"ULID Generation" — entity PKs use `idgen.New()`, not `core.NewULID()`.

## Plan reference

Direct fix:

1. Rename the type + every reference (callers, test files, mocks).
2. Switch `InitGameID` to `idgen.New()`.
3. Roll in the `d0tn.20` close-error-handling fix.
4. Update the stale comment at `internal/cluster/registry.go:71` ("production uses `ulid.Make()`") if it still misrepresents the live code (`registry.go:97` already uses `idgen.New`).

## TDD acceptance criteria

- `task test` passes after rename (no broken imports).
- A unit test asserts `InitGameID` returns IDs generated via `idgen.New` (not `core.NewULID`).
- An existing test exercising `Close()` error paths passes (subsumes `d0tn.20`).

## Verification steps

1. `task test` and `task test:int` green.
2. `rg "PostgresEventStore"` returns zero hits.
3. `task pr-prep` green.
4. After landing: `bd close d0tn.20 --reason "superseded by dj95.8"`.

## Files touched

- `internal/store/postgres.go` (rename + idgen)
- Every caller of `PostgresEventStore` (likely `cmd/holomush/core.go`, test files)
- Mocks under `internal/mocks/` or similar
- `internal/cluster/registry.go:71` (stale comment) if applicable

## Dependencies

- None.
- Cross-link: `holomush-d0tn.20` — folds into this bead per triage §D7.

## Out of scope

- Re-shaping `SystemInfo` itself.
- Migrating the gameID storage to a different backend.
notes: Cross-link per triage §D7: holomush-d0tn.20 (PostgresEventStore.Close error handling, P2 OPEN) overlaps the rename target file. Fold the close-error-handling fix INTO this bead's PR (one commit). After this bead lands, run: bd close holomush-d0tn.20 --reason 'superseded by holomush-dj95.8'. Materialization gate: do not close d0tn.20 prematurely — wait until dj95.8 is actually merged.

title: Delete decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch + its callsites (depends on dj95.1)
description:
## Goal

Delete the legacy `decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch` function (`internal/eventbus/history/dispatcher.go:252-378`) and all production callsites that reach it. After `dj95.1` plumbs the resolver-aware path everywhere, this function has no production callers.

## Design reference

- Audit: `docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/architecture-review.md` §5.1, §8.P0.1
- Triage: `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-14-architecture-cleanup-triage.md` §D4, Wave 1
- Direction: "complete the holomush-ojw1 follow-up... then delete `decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch`."

## Plan reference

Direct implementation; no separate plan.

1. Confirm `dj95.1` has landed (every production caller routes through `DispatchFor`).
2. Delete the function body, the helpers it uniquely uses, and the dead fallback branches in `cold_postgres.go` / `hot_jetstream.go`.
3. Remove or relocate any test that called the legacy function directly.

## TDD acceptance criteria

- After deletion, `rg "decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch" internal/` returns nothing.
- Full `task test` + `task test:int` suite green.
- INV-19, INV-39, AuthGuard denial, AUDIT_QUEUE_FULL zeroization tests all still pass through the surviving `DispatchFor` codepath.

## Verification steps

1. `task test` and `task test:int` both green.
2. `rg "decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch"` returns zero hits.
3. `task pr-prep` green.

## Files touched

- `internal/eventbus/history/dispatcher.go` (delete function + helpers)
- `internal/eventbus/history/cold_postgres.go` (remove `d == nil` fallback branch)
- `internal/eventbus/history/hot_jetstream.go` (remove `d == nil` fallback branch)
- Test files exercising the legacy function

## Dependencies

- Blocks on: `dj95.1` (cold-tier auth plumbing).

## Out of scope

- Renaming `DispatchFor` or refactoring its API.
- Changes to AuthGuard semantics.
notes:
RE-TRIAGE NEEDED (blocker dj95.1 closed as resolved-elsewhere 2026-05-29). dj95.2 cannot delete decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch cleanly: it remains the live fallback for crypto-DISABLED deployments at cold_postgres.go:451 and hot_jetstream.go:594. With Crypto.Enabled=false a supported config, the function is still load-bearing (identity-codec passthrough). Options: (a) rescope to 'remove production-crypto fallback only' — effectively already done via jxo8.7.44, so this may be a no-op/close; or (b) gate full deletion on a separate decision to make crypto mandatory. Do NOT execute as a blind deletion.
TRIAGE RESULT (2026-05-29): VALID but MIS-SCOPED. Bead premise 'no production callers after dj95.1' is FALSE — 3 callers: hot_jetstream.go:594 + cold_postgres.go:451 (crypto-disabled/resolver==nil path) AND readback.go:133 (decryptPluginRow — ALWAYS uses it, runs in crypto-ENABLED prod for plugin read-back/snapshot+fence). NOT a security bypass: decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch and DispatchFor run identical authz+audit; only diff is DEK source (inline dekMgr.Resolve vs resolver.Resolve+INV-39 fallback). Real value = dedup ~120 lines of hand-synced security-critical authz/audit logic (drift = latent fail-open). HIDDEN BLOCKER: DispatchFor's SessionCheckRequest does NOT set ReadBack (dispatcher.go:101-107), so it cannot yet absorb the readback.go caller. CORRECTED SCOPE: (1) add readBack param + SimpleResolver/nil-resolver path to DispatchFor so it subsumes all 3 cases; (2) reroute hot+cold+readback through DispatchFor; (3) delete decodeAuthorizeAndDispatch (rg=0); (4) crypto-reviewer + task test:int (INV-19/39, AuthGuard denial, AUDIT_QUEUE_FULL, INV-RB-3). Priority P2 (arguably P3) — refactor, no active bug. Supersedes the description's Goal/Plan/'no callers' acceptance criterion.

title: Command State Management for Multi-Turn Interactions
description:
## Context

Many MUSH commands benefit from multi-turn interactions:

- **Confirmation dialogs**: "Are you sure you want to delete this room? (yes/no)"
- **Wizards**: Step-by-step object creation with prompts for name, description, attributes
- **Editors**: In-line text editing sessions for descriptions
- **Menus**: Numbered option selection for complex commands

HoloMUSH uses gopher-lua for Lua plugin commands. Each command invocation creates a
fresh Lua VM state that is discarded after execution. This design choice provides:

- Isolation between command invocations (no accidental state leakage)
- Predictable behavior (same input always produces same output)
- Simple concurrency model (no shared mutable state)
- Fast startup (~50μs per VM vs ~1.5s for WASM)

However, it means Lua commands cannot maintain state between invocations. A "wizard"
command cannot remember which step the user is on.

## Decision

**Deferred to post-v1.** For the initial release, multi-turn command wizards are
explicitly out of scope. Commands MUST be designed as single-turn interactions.

For v1, workarounds include:

1. **Confirmation flags**: Use `--confirm` or `--force` flags for destructive actions
   instead of interactive confirmation dialogs.

2. **Single-command completion**: Design commands to gather all input at once:
   `@create room "The Library"="A dusty room filled with books."`

3. **Host function KV store** (if implemented): Use session-scoped storage for
   commands that absolutely need state between invocations.

Post-v1, the recommended approach is **Option B: Session-Scoped State Store** because:

- It provides the cleanest API for plugin authors
- It aligns with existing session management infrastructure
- It avoids client-side complexity of continuation tokens
- It maintains the isolation benefits of fresh Lua VMs

## Rationale

_See Decision section above. The original ADR did not have a separate Rationale section._

## Alternatives Considered

### Option A: Continuation Tokens

Commands return a continuation token in their response. The next command invocation
includes this token, allowing the handler to reconstruct state.

```lua
function handle_command(event)
    local state = decode_continuation(event.continuation_token)
    if state.step == 1 then
        return {
            output = "Enter room name:",
            continuation = encode_continuation({step = 2})
        }
    end
end
```

**Pros:**

- Stateless server, all state in token
- Works with load balancing

**Cons:**

- Tokens can be replayed, forged, or tampered with
- Requires cryptographic signing and validation
- Large tokens for complex state
- Client must return token with next input

### Option B: Session-Scoped State Store

Host functions provide a key-value store scoped to the player's session:

```lua
function handle_command(event)
    local step = host.session_get("create_room_step") or 1
    if step == 1 then
        host.session_set("create_room_step", 2)
        return {output = "Enter room name:"}
    elseif step == 2 then
        -- Continue wizard flow...
        return {output = "Enter room description:"}
    end
end
```

**Pros:**

- Simple API for plugin authors
- State persists across commands within session
- No client cooperation required

**Cons:**

- Requires session state storage (in-memory or database)
- State cleanup on session end
- Potential for orphaned state if command flow is interrupted

### Option C: Go State Machines

Multi-turn commands are implemented in Go with explicit state machines:

```go
type CreateRoomWizard struct {
    step    int
    name    string
    desc    string
}

func (w *CreateRoomWizard) HandleInput(input string) (output string, done bool) {
    // State machine logic
}
```

**Pros:**

- Full control over state management
- Type-safe state transitions
- Can use Go's concurrency primitives

**Cons:**

- Defeats purpose of Lua plugins for customization
- More boilerplate for simple interactions

### Option D: Lua State Pooling

Maintain a pool of Lua VMs per plugin, reusing them for the same session:

**Pros:**

- Lua state naturally persists within VM
- Familiar programming model

**Cons:**

- Complex lifecycle management
- Memory pressure from many concurrent sessions
- State leakage between different commands in same session
- Defeats isolation benefits of fresh VMs

## Consequences

### v1 Implementations MUST NOT

- Assume Lua state persists between command invocations
- Implement multi-turn wizards that depend on Lua-side state
- Store command progress in global Lua variables
- Use Lua coroutines expecting they survive command completion

### v1 Implementations SHOULD

- Use confirmation flags (`--confirm`, `--force`) for destructive operations
- Accept all required input in a single command invocation
- Document single-turn limitations in plugin authoring guides

### Future Work

When implementing session-scoped state (post-v1):

- Define host function API: `session_get(key)`, `session_set(key, value)`, `session_delete(key)`
- Implement state cleanup on session termination
- Add TTL support for automatic state expiration
- Consider state size limits to prevent abuse

## References

- [Commands & Behaviors Design](../specs/2026-02-02-commands-behaviors-design.md) - Parent spec
- [Plugin System Design](../specs/2026-01-18-plugin-system-design.md) - Lua VM architecture
- Implementation: `internal/plugin/lua/host.go`

Legacy ADR number: 0005

title: Repo audit follow-up: codebase humanization / de-slop (2026-05-13)
description:
## Goal

Refine the humanization / anti-AI-slop findings from the 2026-05-13 repository audit into discrete cleanup beads. Each category (comment slop, defensive overengineering, verbose-by-default Go, markdown slop, test slop, doc rot) becomes one or more child beads. Epic closes when all children close or are explicitly deferred.

## Audit report

`docs/repository-audit/2026-05-13/humanization-review.md`

Top headline findings (see report for full list and quoted offending text):
- `AGENTS.md` and `CLAUDE.md` are byte-identical 21 KB twins — consolidate to a single source.
- `internal/world/service_test.go` repeats the same 3-line setup 226 times — table-driven refactor candidate.
- `internal/access/prefix.go` has eight near-duplicate panic-on-empty helpers each with 4-line docstrings.
- `NewX` / `NewXWithLogger` constructor pairs in `internal/auth/` duplicate 30-line nil-check blocks.
- Seven plans in `docs/plans/` still claim `Ready for implementation` for work that shipped in Q1.
- One file in `docs/superpowers/plans/` contains 265 em-dashes.

The auditor's overall read: the codebase is in better shape than typical AI-assisted projects — this is gardening, not rescue.

## Triage approach

1. **Recommended cadence: rolling cleanup PRs, not a mega-PR.** One child bead per category, scoped small enough to land independently. The audit's "Quick wins" section lists 20 high-impact targets — start there.
2. Each child bead MUST follow the 8-section format and cite `path:line` evidence from the audit report.
3. `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` consolidation is its own bead — touches every agent's discovery path, so it needs a brief design note before edit.
4. Plan-status doc rot can be batched into a single sweep.

## Out of scope

- Architecture / layering / spec drift — tracked in sibling epics. If a humanization finding turns out to be load-bearing structural work, kick it to the appropriate sibling.
- Style-only changes that don't reduce LoC or improve clarity. The point is fewer lines doing the same work, not aesthetic preference.

title: Phase 3b INV-22/25 history decrypt integration tests
description:
## Goal

Write integration tests for two history-decrypt invariants that were deferred from Phase 3b's T12 priority-3 list:

- **INV-22** — QueryStreamHistory parity: decrypt-on-fanout must behave identically for hot-tier subscribe and cold-tier history reads.
- **INV-25** — AAD tamper detection on the history path: cold-tier rows with tampered AAD must surface metadata-only or denial, not decrypt to plaintext.

## Why this exists

Phase 3b shipped without these two integration tests because the setup requires the full QueryStreamHistory handler plumbing + hot-tier AuthGuard wiring, which only finished during Phase 3d. The work item was tracked as ojw1.2.14 (closed) under sub-epic B's epic.

The substrate is now in place: `internal/eventbus/history/hot_jetstream.go` exposes `decodeAndAuthorizeHistory` and `decodeViaResolver`, and Phase 5 sub-epic F exercises the history path under real DEK lifecycle. INV-22 still lacks a dedicated parity test pointer in the spec invariant table; INV-25 has codec-level coverage but no history-path coverage.

## Reference

- docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-25-event-payload-crypto-design.md §2 (invariant table — INV-22/25 entries)
- docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-02-event-payload-crypto-phase3b-authguard-decrypt.md T12 (the deferred priority-3 row)

## TDD acceptance criteria

TBD by writing-plans. At minimum: two new integration tests under `test/integration/crypto/` covering (a) hot/cold parity on a sensitive event after rotation, (b) AAD-tampered cold-tier row surfaces metadata-only.

## Dependencies

None — substrate is in place.

## History

Re-filed from holomush-ojw1.2.14 (closed) to detach from the ojw1.2 epic so Phase 3b can be considered complete. The work itself is unchanged P2 test-coverage gap.

title: TestMigrator_ConcurrentUp flaky on docker port-map timeout
description:
## Goal
Diagnose and fix the testcontainers race where `internal/store.TestMigrator_ConcurrentUp` times out waiting for the PostgreSQL container's port mapping during startup.

## Symptom
`task pr-prep` failure observed 2026-05-09 in worktree `config-grooming` with this error:

```
=== FAIL: internal/store TestMigrator_ConcurrentUp (63.28s)
    migrate_integration_test.go:262: 
        Error: Received unexpected error:
        run postgres: generic container: start container: started hook: 
        wait until ready: mapped port: check target: retries: 9, port: "", 
        last err: get state: Get "http://127.0.0.1:49900/v1.51/containers/<id>/json": 
        context deadline exceeded
```

The test was unrelated to the diff under test (a `.claude/`-only PR). Docker daemon was healthy when checked post-failure (`docker ps` returned 0 containers; `docker version` returned 29.4.2).

## TDD acceptance criteria
- MUST identify whether this is (a) testcontainers timeout default too tight for this codepath, (b) a real race in migrator startup, or (c) a Docker daemon resource issue
- MUST NOT just bump retries / timeouts without root-cause analysis (per `feedback_no_flakes_investigate`)
- SHOULD add a deterministic reproducer (e.g., resource-constrained container start) if the timeout is the root cause

## Verification steps
1. `task test:int -- ./internal/store/ -run TestMigrator_ConcurrentUp -count=10` — does it flake on a clean Docker?
2. Inspect testcontainers BasicWaitStrategies vs ForLog/ForExposedPort for postgres
3. Check docker desktop / colima resource caps if local-machine-specific

## Files touched
- `internal/store/migrate_integration_test.go` (likely; investigation TBD)
- possibly `internal/store/migrate.go` if a real concurrent-init bug surfaces

## Dependencies
- None

## Out of scope
- Fixing other testcontainers flakes — file separately if encountered
notes: Re-run on 2026-05-09 in same worktree passed cleanly: internal/store integration tests took 35.114s (vs 1m34.976s when it failed); test/integration/eventbus_e2e took 1m11s. Confirms transient nature — likely Docker port-map race during container start. Investigation should reproduce by running test:int -count=10 under resource pressure or with concurrent test runs.

title: Wire KEK provider into production core.go (mirror admin CLI's file-source pattern)
description:
**Goal:** Replicate the file-source KEK construction from `cmd/holomush/cmd_admin_totp_deps.go::buildKEKProviderFromConfig` into `cmd/holomush/core.go`'s `runCoreWithDeps` so the production server has a KEK provider available for DEK wrap/unwrap operations.

**Why:** Phase 5 sub-epic A T12 (holomush-jxo8.3.12) created the FIRST production KEK wiring, scoped to the admin CLI. The core server currently does NOT construct a kek.Provider. Until both paths use the SAME source (file path + passphrase), wrapped TOTP secrets created via admin CLI cannot be unwrapped by the running server, and DEKs created server-side cannot be unwrapped by admin CLI.

**Constraint:** Production paths MUST use file-source (not env-source — env-source has `prodMode=true` rejection at `internal/eventbus/crypto/kek/source_env.go:44-48`). Use the same env vars as the admin CLI: `HOLOMUSH_KEK_FILE` + `HOLOMUSH_KEK_PASSPHRASE`. Future work can layer config-driven selection on top.

**Files:**
- `cmd/holomush/core.go` (modify — add KEK provider construction near the existing pool setup, before any DEK manager construction)
- Possibly `cmd/holomush/cmd_admin_totp_deps.go` (refactor — share `buildKEKProviderFromConfig` as a package-level helper if both paths can call it directly)

**Verification:** Round-trip test (file-source) — wrap a secret via admin CLI, unwrap via server (or vice versa). Existing crypto E2E tests likely cover this once both paths land.

**Out of scope:** Adding env-source support for production (refused by the package), config-driven source selection, vault-source.
notes: Triage 2026-05-24 (KEK-WARN investigation): this task APPEARS ALREADY IMPLEMENTED. cmd/holomush/core.go:689 calls buildKEKProviderFromConfig (cmd_admin_totp_deps.go:102-126) using the same env vars as the admin CLI (HOLOMUSH_KEK_FILE + HOLOMUSH_KEK_PASSPHRASE), file-source, and threads kekProvider into totp.NewService (core.go:697), rekey wiring (buildRekeyWiring, core.go:~790), and readstream wiring. This matches q825's described scope. NOT closing without grounded proof — recommend running the admin-CLI<->server KEK round-trip (wrap a secret via admin CLI, unwrap via server) or the existing crypto E2E before closing. Co-discovered: holomush-iw804 (dev-boot WARN log hygiene), holomush-qqigi (prod fail-closed design decision).

title: Reserved plugin names — block 'system' / 'world-service' from manifest validation
description: Follow-up from holomush-w9ml.6 code review: bootstrap loop checks sentinel-ULID collision but not sentinel-NAME collision. A plugin named 'system' or 'world-service' would silently overwrite the sentinel entry in activeByName, violating the IdentityRegistry-doesn't-resolve-sentinel-labels invariant at runtime. Add reserved-name denylist to internal/plugin/manifest.go::Validate (and/or guard the bootstrap loop). namePattern at manifest.go:303 currently allows both. Code-reviewer report: .claude/agent-memory/code-reviewer/reports/2026-05-04-2006-holomush-w9ml-T5.md

title: Flaky test: TestCoordinatorRequestInvalidationReturnsRateLimitedWhenProbeAndPillRefuses
description:
## Problem

`internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation/coordinator_send_test.go:57` fails intermittently in CI, returning nil where `INVALIDATION_RATE_LIMITED` is expected.

## Evidence

- **Failing run:** PR #3527 CI Test job — https://github.com/holomush/holomush/actions/runs/25322231824/job/74233864488
- **Failure mode:**
  ```
  === FAIL: internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation TestCoordinatorRequestInvalidationReturnsRateLimitedWhenProbeAndPillRefuses (0.33s)
      Error Trace:	pkg/errutil/testing.go:18
      Error:      	Should be true
      Messages:   	expected oops error, got <nil>
  ```
- **Same test passing on `main`:** Last 5 CI runs on main are all green (PR #3517, #3518, #3519, #3520, #3521).
- **PR scope:** PR #3527 only touches `.claude/` automation files + CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md — zero Go changes. The failure cannot have been introduced by this PR.

## Probable race

Test relies on the second `RequestInvalidation` call landing inside the rate-limit window opened by the first. On a heavily-loaded CI runner, the second call can arrive after the rate-limit window has expired, in which case `ProbeAndPill` doesn't return `CLUSTER_PILL_RATE_LIMITED`, the retry phase succeeds, and `coord.RequestInvalidation` returns `nil` instead of `ErrRateLimited`.

Relevant code paths:
- `internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation/coordinator.go:160-205` — `ProbeAndPill` dispatch + rate-limit branch
- `internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation/coordinator.go:207-219` — retry phase (the path that succeeds when rate-limit window has lapsed)
- `internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation/coordinator_send_test.go:95-112` — first call result is discarded; second call's rate-limit assertion is the entire failure surface

The test uses `InvalidateTimeout: 200ms` and re-injects a synthetic heartbeat between the two calls, but does NOT pin the rate-limit clock or assert that the second call lands inside the window.

## Possible fixes

1. **Inject a clock seam.** Replace wall-clock `time.Now()` in the rate-limit logic with an injectable clock; pin it for the test.
2. **Make the rate-limit window configurable per-test** and set it long enough (e.g., 30s) that scheduling jitter on CI cannot push the second call out of the window.
3. **Assert "rate-limited OR succeeded-without-retry"** if the test is really just checking that we don't double-pill — but this weakens the assertion.

Option 1 is the cleanest; option 2 is the lowest-cost; option 3 changes test semantics.

## Cross-refs

- Project memory: `feedback_no_flakes_investigate` — diagnosis required before re-running CI; this bead captures that diagnosis.
- `holomush-ojw1.3` epic — invalidation package was introduced by PR #3519 (Phase 3c).

comments.author: Sean Brandt
comments.text:
Consolidating 4 duplicate beads — all the SAME flaky test (internal/eventbus/crypto/invalidation/coordinator_send_test.go :: TestCoordinatorRequestInvalidationReturnsRateLimitedWhenProbeAndPillRefuses); all timing/gossip/rate-limit races, differing only by which assertion trips:
- yjed (2026-05-04, canonical): nil where INVALIDATION_RATE_LIMITED expected (:57), PR #3527
- holomush-0gruo (2026-06-01): 'Should be true' then passed on re-run, PR #4360
- holomush-mljp4 (2026-06-05): 'expected oops error, got nil' (errutil/testing.go:18), PR #4382
- holomush-js25w (2026-06-20): AwaitMemberPresent gossip race under -race
- holomush-g6dq8 (2026-06-26): AwaitMemberAbsent 5s timeout (:93); passed 5/5 in isolation
Closing the other four as dupes of this one. STRONG QUARANTINE CANDIDATE: flaked 5+ times over ~2 months on unrelated PRs — prefer test/quarantine.yaml + marker over continued re-filing.

title: Formalize feature-flag/gate system across the host
description:
The codebase has accumulated several ad-hoc feature flags scattered across config structs without a unified pattern: AccessControl staged migration, JetStream cutover phases, and now Crypto.Enabled (Phase 3a, holomush-ojw1.1.8). Each is a one-off bool with no shared metadata, no naming convention, no observability hook, and no audit trail of who flipped what when.

What 'formalize' means for this bead:
- Single FeatureFlag type with metadata (name, default, description, owner, expected-removal-date).
- Registry that knows every flag at startup; refuses unknown lookups (defends against typos that silently default to false).
- Source layering: defaults < env vars < config file < admin override (last wins).
- Observability: emit a metric per (flag_name, value) at startup; emit an audit event when a flag flips at runtime.
- Documentation pattern: every flag links to the bead/PR that introduced it AND the bead/PR that's expected to remove it. 'Permanent' flags are the exception, not the default.
- Migration: convert existing ad-hoc flags (Crypto.Enabled, any AccessControl mode toggles, any JetStream tier flags) onto the new system as part of landing it.

Why now: Phase 3a introduces Crypto.Enabled with no plan for its lifecycle. Phase 3d will flip it. Phases 4-7 will introduce more crypto-related toggles. Without a shared pattern, they all become per-PR ad-hoc decisions.

Out of scope: real dynamic-config / Unleash / LaunchDarkly integration. v1 is a typed in-process registry — dynamic toggles via admin socket are a future bead.

title: DX: task dev requires full Docker rebuild — add backend-only iteration loop
description:
## Finding

`task dev` requires a full Docker image rebuild on every run.

- `Taskfile.yaml:23-27` — `dev` unconditionally calls `docker:build`, which runs `web:embed` (full pnpm SvelteKit build) and cross-compiles all binary plugins for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64.
- Changing one Go file to test the live server takes several minutes for a rebuild that doesn't need the SvelteKit bundle or cross-arch plugins.
- No `task dev:go` or equivalent that runs `go run ./cmd/holomush core ...` directly against a Postgres container started separately.

## Why it matters

`task test -- ./internal/foo/` works for unit-testable changes. Verifying behavior against a live server (interactive telnet, web flows, plugin reload semantics) requires the full Docker rebuild cycle. This is the single biggest daily-loop friction the devops review identified.

A `task dev:go` that brings up Postgres (and optionally NATS) via compose, then runs the server with hot-reload-by-restart, would unblock fast iteration for backend-only contributors.

## Severity

P2 (developer experience). Affects every contributor working on the Go backend.

title: Build/CI: pinning discipline outliers — corepack pnpm, arduino/setup-task, compose images
description:
## Finding

The project's SHA-pinning discipline is nearly complete (every GitHub Action SHA-pinned with version comment). Three outliers.

### corepack pnpm@latest in CI
- `ci.yaml:205`, `ci.yaml:253`, `release.yaml:92` — `corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate`. Resolves at run-time to whatever corepack considers latest. A pnpm major bump (lockfile format change) can fail builds without any code change.
- Pin to the version matching `web/pnpm-lock.yaml`'s lockfileVersion.

### arduino/setup-task@v2 not SHA-pinned
- Used in `ci.yaml`, `benchmark-check.yml`, `nightly-soak.yml`, `release.yaml` — only action without commit-SHA pin. Other actions all use `@<sha> # v<n>` pattern. actionlint does not enforce SHA pinning, so it passes today.
- Pin to a SHA; `.github/renovate.json` will auto-maintain.

### Compose observability images use `:latest@sha256:`
- `compose.yaml:69, 82, 95, 104, 122` — `otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:latest@sha256:...`, `jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest@sha256:...`, etc. SHA pin makes content fixed but `:latest` tag misleads.
- Renovate's Docker bumping cannot determine whether the digest represents a newer version when the tag is `:latest`. Use a version tag (e.g. `0.122.1@sha256:...`) so Renovate can do its job.

## Severity

P2 (build reliability + reproducibility).

title: Build/CI: add drift detection — mocks, proto codegen, buf breaking-change
description:
## Finding

CI catches lint, format, license headers, schema drift — but not three categories of generated-code drift.

### Mock drift
- mockery generates from interfaces (`.mockery.yaml`). If an interface changes and `task mocks:generate` is not run, drift is caught only at compile time in tests.
- Add a CI step: `mockery --dry-run` or compare generated mock checksum to HEAD.

### Proto codegen drift
- `pkg/proto/` and `internal/eventbus/cursor/cursorv1/` contain committed `.pb.go` files. CI never verifies they are current.
- The Taskfile `proto:` task has correct `sources`/`generates` definitions; CI just doesn't run a drift check.
- Add: `buf generate && buf generate --template buf.gen.internal.yaml && git diff --exit-code pkg/proto/ internal/eventbus/cursor/cursorv1/`.

### buf breaking-change detection disabled
- `buf.yml:37` has `breaking: false`. Backward-incompatible proto changes (field removals, type changes, reserved-number reuse) can land silently.
- Set `breaking: true` with `against: ".git#branch=main"`.

## Why it matters

Generated code that is committed but never drift-checked is one of the highest sources of "works on my machine" surprise. Each gate is a 10-second CI step.

## Severity

P2 (CI completeness).

title: Build: repo hygiene — dual renovate.json, tracked lock file, stray issues.jsonl
description:
## Finding

Three small repo-hygiene issues that signal "unmaintained" on first browse.

### Two divergent renovate.json files
- `/renovate.json` (root) — uses `config:base` with only NATS-specific rules (`minimumReleaseAge: 14 days`)
- `/.github/renovate.json` — uses `config:best-practices` with auto-merge, grouping, timezone, rate-limiting

`.github/renovate.json` takes precedence. The root file is stale and confusing. The NATS minimumReleaseAge rule is not in the active config — verify whether that protection should be ported.

### Tracked runtime lock file
- `.claude/scheduled_tasks.lock` is tracked in git and currently shows as deleted (`D` status). Lock files for runtime state should not be in VCS at all. `.gitignore` does not cover this path.

### Stray files in working tree
- `/issues.jsonl` is in the working tree at review time. `.gitignore:24` already names it explicitly as "Stray beads export in worktree root." Not tracked, but present.

## Severity

P2 (repo hygiene). Each one-line fix.

title: Docs: per-package doc.go for high-traffic packages + plugin developer onboarding docs
description:
## Finding

Per-package doc.go is essentially absent across internal/. Only internal/audit/doc.go and internal/auth/doc.go exist out of 27 internal packages.

### Highest-traffic packages without package docs
- internal/eventbus
- internal/world
- internal/plugin
- internal/access
- internal/core
- internal/command

### Plugin developer experience gaps
- plugins/core-scenes, plugins/echo-bot, plugins/setting-crossroads, plugins/core-aliases, plugins/core-building, plugins/core-communication, plugins/core-help, plugins/core-objects, plugins/test-abac-widget — none have a README.md, only plugin.yaml
- No top-level plugins/README.md explaining what each subdirectory is, which are first-party "core-*" examples, which is the test fixture
- site/docs/extending/binary-plugins.md jumps to manifest without a "compile + register + reload" walkthrough analogous to the Lua getting-started.md

## Why it matters

`go doc` and godoc are the natural entry points for code-reading contributors. With no package docs, the architectural intent of each package lives only in CLAUDE.md and the docs site — not at the IDE/tool layer.

The plugin developer story is the project's stated extensibility pitch; a third party browsing GitHub today gets no map of what each plugin is or how to write a binary one from scratch.

## Severity

P2 (docs / contributor onboarding).

title: Docs/code: terminology sweep — room still in canonical command help and player-facing strings
description:
## Finding

CLAUDE.md mandates location/exit/character/player/session/connection terminology. The word "room" persists in user-visible places.

### Canonical command help (reaches the player UI)

- internal/plugin/help_integration_test.go:35,51,52,220,232-234,380 — "Say something to the room", "Create a new room or exit", "<room name>"
- internal/command/types_test.go:28,225,235 — same "Say something to the room" string used as test fixture

### Source comments and fixtures

- internal/audit/logger.go:37 — comment uses permit-write-own-room as example slug
- internal/access/policy/engine_test.go:1981 — DSL example: resource == "location:special-room"
- internal/telnet/gateway_handler_test.go:1199-1200 — fixture text "You see a large room."

### Player-facing site docs

- site/docs/guide/the-world.md:44 — "Builders expand the world from here — dig new rooms, link exits"
- site/docs/extending/plugin-guide.md and getting-started.md — "respond to say events in the room" in Lua examples

## Why it matters

CLAUDE.md says terminology MUST NOT mix. The most concerning sites are the help_integration_test.go strings — these are the canonical command-help strings displayed to players. A direct violation that ships to users.

## Severity

P2 (correctness, contributor consistency, player visibility).

title: Docs: site/docs/contributing/architecture.md describes pre-cutover model (stale)
description:
## Finding

Architecture documentation is post-JetStream-cutover stale.

- site/docs/contributing/architecture.md:99 — uses location:<id> colon-style subjects; cutover replaced these with events.<game>.<domain>.<id>
- docs/specs/2026-04-03-channels-architecture.md:45 — uses channel:<channel_id> despite cutover
- site/docs/contributing/event-delivery.md — labeled "(superseded)" in the index but still listed as a key reference; banner-mark at top or move
- site/docs/contributing/index.md:45 — points to architecture.md, propagating the staleness

## Why it matters

The contributor docs landing page directs newcomers at a document that describes architecture the codebase no longer has. The JetStream cutover (PR #252) was the largest change in months; the public-facing docs do not reflect it.

## Cross-cutting

- Pairs with ec22.2 (collapse core.Event/eventbus.Event) and ec22.3 (subjectxlate decision) — refresh after those resolve so the doc describes the destination, not the in-flight state.
- Spec docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-18-jetstream-event-log-design.md is the source of truth for the new model.

## Severity

P2 (docs accuracy).

title: Tests: ~20 string-match error assertions should use error codes (errutil.AssertErrorCode / errors.Is)
description:
## Finding

CLAUDE.md mandates errutil.AssertErrorCode or assert.ErrorIs over string matching on err.Error(). The store package and a few session tests still use the prohibited form.

### Sites
- internal/store/postgres_test.go:82 — `assert.Contains(t, err.Error(), tt.errMsg)` (and surrounding patterns)
- internal/store/player_session_store_test.go — multiple sites (~6 "happy path" subtests with similar pattern)
- internal/store/alias_test.go — multiple sites
- internal/session/memstore_test.go:36, 41 — `assert.Error` only (no specific error type checked) at TestMemStore_Get_NotFound; line 483 uses `assert.Contains(t, err.Error(), "session not found")` for TestMemStore_UpdateFocusMemberships_NotFound

Total: ~20 sites across the three store test files plus the memstore tests.

## Why it matters

- These tests pass if error message phrasing changes but semantics break.
- Coupled with the error-style consistency bead (sibling ec22.10) — to migrate to errors.Is or AssertErrorCode, the underlying errors must carry codes/sentinels in the first place.
- Test-honesty verdict was RIGOROUS overall, with this as the largest pattern-level dent.

## Severity

P2 (test rigor / brittleness).

title: Tests: replace ~16 time.Sleep async-sync sites with deterministic patterns
description:
## Finding

The single biggest test-quality liability the review found. ~16 time.Sleep calls used to synchronize async behavior in tests — flaky on slow CI, wasteful on fast CI, and the canonical anti-pattern CLAUDE.md test guidance is meant to prevent.

### Concentrated sites

- internal/control/grpc_server_test.go — 12 callsites; e.g. line 75 `time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)` after client.Shutdown then `assert.True(t, shutdownCalled.Load())`. Six follow exact "launch goroutine, sleep, check" shape. line 105 also sleeps 50ms "to give server time to start" before gRPC dial.
- internal/telnet/gateway_handler_test.go:2377, 2384, 2438, 2443 — four 400ms sleeps to "give the handler time to print the banner." Expensive in parallel CI.
- internal/audit/logger_test.go:101 — 50ms sleep to confirm Allow event in MinimalMode produces no async writes.
- internal/content/postgres_store_test.go:118 — additional sleep site flagged.

### Existing precedent

CLAUDE.md memory project_cursor_lock_finding_1 documents the cursorCommitHook test seam pattern (channel-based ready signal + atomic poll). The pattern works; it just hasn't propagated to these older tests.

## Why it matters

CLAUDE.md test guidance explicitly disallows fixed-sleep async sync. require.Eventually, channel-signaled ready hooks, or atomic-bool polling all give deterministic behavior. Fixing these is the highest-ROI test-trustworthiness fix.

## Severity

P2 (test reliability).

title: Code quality: magic values + silent cursor-encode failures
description:
## Finding

Two small code-quality items violating CLAUDE.md / project-memory rules.

### Magic values in audit
- internal/audit/postgres.go:34-37 — make(chan Event, 1000), batchSize: 100, flushPeriod: 1 * time.Second hardcoded in NewPostgresWriter. CLAUDE.md memory feedback_no_magic_values says "always use enum constants and named values." Should be exported/named consts (defaultAuditChanBuf, defaultAuditBatchSize, defaultAuditFlushPeriod).

### Mixed time-shaped config types
- cmd/holomush/core.go:54-56 — SessionTTL string, SessionReaperInterval string, but LuaTimeout time.Duration. Three time-shaped fields, two string-typed (parsed later) and one already typed. Inconsistent and a parse-error trap.

### Silent cursor-encode failures
- internal/grpc/query_stream_history.go:397-399 and 415-417 — `if err != nil { return nil }` swallows cursor-encoding failures with only a comment. A misconfigured cursor schema becomes a silent nil-cursor. At minimum: slog.WarnContext(ctx, ...) so the failure is observable.

## Severity

P2 (code quality). Three small items grouped because each is one-spot.

title: Code quality: hostfunc context-fallback duplicated 13+ sites — apply existing helpers
description:
## Finding

The pattern `parentCtx := L.Context(); if parentCtx == nil { parentCtx = context.Background() }; ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(...)` is reimplemented at 13+ sites despite the helpers existing.

### Helpers (already present)
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/helpers.go:80-94 — withQueryContext
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/helpers.go:101-120 — withMutatorContext

### Duplication sites (sample)
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/functions.go (multiple)
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/stdlib_streams.go
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/stdlib_session.go
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/stdlib_focus.go
- internal/plugin/hostfunc/commands.go

## Why it matters

The helper was added partway through and not back-applied — classic incomplete refactor. The bare pattern's nil check exists because L.Context() can be nil during plugin lifecycle edges; future contributors copying the bare pattern will keep replicating it.

## Severity

P2 (code quality / DRY). Mechanical sweep.

title: Code quality: standardize on oops at boundaries — three packages still use bare fmt.Errorf
description:
## Finding

CLAUDE.md mandates oops.Code/With/Wrap at API boundaries. The codebase complies almost everywhere, with three concentrated drift sites:

### internal/access/policy/store/postgres.go
- Lines 54, 79, 90, 454 — bare fmt.Errorf alongside oops.Code("POLICY_INVALID_AST") in the same file. Drift within one file.

### internal/plugin/schema_provisioner.go
- Lines 68, 238, 244, 249, 256, 261 — mixes oops.With(...).Wrap(fmt.Errorf(...)) with bare fmt.Errorf.

### internal/eventbus/types.go + internal/eventbus/codec/registry.go
- types.go:95,99,102,118; codec/registry.go:29 — fmt.Errorf + sentinel pattern at exactly the boundary plugin code crosses, while the host re-wraps via oops at cmd/holomush/sub_grpc.go:447.

### internal/audit/postgres.go:94
- fmt.Errorf("async channel full") is a string-only error (no %w, no oops code). Callers cannot match via errors.Is or errutil.AssertErrorCode. Should be a sentinel ErrAuditChannelFull or oops code.

## Why it matters

- Inconsistent error style at exactly the points contributors look first when debugging.
- Test-quality reviewer also flagged ~20 string-match assertions on errors that depend on this consistency to fix.
- errutil.AssertErrorCode pattern only works if the error has a code.

## Severity

P2 (code quality / consistency). Mechanical.

title: Auth audit trail completeness: thread UA/IP through web+telnet to AuthenticatePlayer; add per-IP caps
description:
## Finding

The auth path drops user-agent and IP context when proxying from web (and likely telnet) into the core gRPC AuthenticatePlayer call.

- internal/web/auth_handlers.go:50-80 — does not extract RemoteAddr / User-Agent / X-Forwarded-For from the HTTP request
- internal/grpc/auth_handlers.go:145 — calls s.authService.AuthenticatePlayer(ctx, ..., "", "") with empty UA/IP fields
- Telnet path likely loses the same context

Separately, the connection cap is global with no per-IP shaping:
- cmd/holomush/gateway.go:478-487 — buffered-channel cap (good); over-cap clients get RefuseOverCapacity. A single hostile peer can exhaust the global slot pool.

## Why it matters

- Failed-login forensics, eviction analysis, and breach review depend on UA/IP being recorded; web clients are degraded for all of these.
- Per-IP caps are the natural follow-on to per-IP threading and would unblock per-IP rate-limit/lockout policy.
- Not directly exploitable; affects incident response posture, not authn/authz correctness.

## Pointers

- internal/web/auth_handlers.go:50-80
- internal/grpc/auth_handlers.go:145
- internal/telnet/<auth-equivalent path>
- cmd/holomush/gateway.go:478-487 (connection cap surface)

## Severity

P2 (security hygiene, not exploit-grade).

title: Plugin runtime hardening: proxy goroutine cleanup, Lua ctx watchdog, mTLS-by-default
description:
## Finding (consolidated plugin runtime hardening basket)

Three medium-severity plugin runtime gaps that share lifecycle/sandbox theming.

### Broker proxy goroutines leak on Init failure
- internal/plugin/goplugin/host.go:402-460 — after grpcPlugin.broker.AcceptAndServe(...) starts proxy goroutines (lines 402, 423), an Init failure path (line 454) calls only client.Kill() and certDir cleanup. Proxy goroutines remain attached to the killed plugin's broker; can panic or leak FDs when the broker connection drops. Limited blast radius (host-only, requires misbehaving plugin) but a plugin that fails Init repeatedly leaks both.

### Lua hostfunc context watchdog
- internal/plugin/lua/invoke.go:35-50 — comment claims "every registered hostfunc either is O(1) or respects L.Context()". That is an audit invariant, not enforced. A future hostfunc that ignores ctx leaves the goroutine pinned forever (<-done blocks). A single misbehaving plugin → memory pressure attack.
- Mitigation could be a 2x cpuTimeout watchdog that abandons the goroutine + records a metric, or a CI lint enforcing ctx-checking in hostfunc/*.go.

### mTLS optional by configuration
- internal/plugin/goplugin/host.go:80-86 — non-CA fallback exists with WARN log. A production deployment that forgets --certs-dir runs unauthenticated plugin gRPC. Anti-pattern even with the warn line.
- Mitigation: make CA mandatory unless an explicit --allow-unauthenticated-plugins flag is set.

## Why it matters

Plugin sandbox is otherwise STRONG (mTLS with TLS 1.3 floor, per-plugin cert dirs at 0o700/0o600, Lua sandbox with SkipOpenLibs and registry caps, EvalSymlinks path-traversal check). These three items are the remaining hardening gaps the security review found at the runtime boundary.

## Cross-cutting

- Lua sandbox itself was rated STRONG; this is the watchdog enforcement layer
- mTLS-by-default complements existing TLS 1.3 enforcement

## Severity

P2 (security hardening, not exploit-grade).

title: Architecture cleanup: dead engine handlers + EventStoreAdapter + manager/server god-objects
description:
## Finding (consolidated cleanup basket)

Several smaller architectural smells that share a common shape: vestigial mediation layers and large modules that grew through accumulation.

### Dead handlers
- internal/core/engine.go:78,101 — HandleSay/HandlePose are called only from engine_test.go. Real say/pose flow through plugins/core-communication. The engine still hard-codes Stream: "location:" + char.LocationID.String() (legacy colon naming). Pretends say/pose still live in the host.

### Dead mediation layer
- internal/world/event_store_adapter.go — wraps a core.EventAppender to add ID/timestamp/system-actor for a one-call-site adapter. Disappears when core.Event collapses (sibling bead ec22.2).

### CoreServer god-struct
- internal/grpc/server.go:121-171, 1331 LOC, 38 functions, ~15 collaborator fields (engine, sessionStore, eventStore, worldQuerier, dispatcher, cmdServices, four auth services, two repos, guestService, streamContributor, streamRegistry, focusCoordinator, accessEngine, eventbus.Subscriber, HistoryReader, gameID provider, two ID generators, VerbRegistry). Auth handlers were already split off (auth_handlers.go); other RPC groups should follow.

### Plugin Manager bloat
- internal/plugin/manager.go, 1222 LOC, 43 functions, 9 ManagerOptions (WithLuaHost, WithPolicyInstaller, WithAliasSeeder, WithAttributeProviderRegistrar, WithAttributeProviderUnregistrar, WithServiceRegistry, WithTrustAllowlist, WithGracefulDegradation, WithVerbRegistry). Probably two distinct responsibilities (load-time wiring vs runtime delivery).
- internal/plugin/manager.go:622-645 — TypeSetting falls through default switch case with "unknown plugin type" log. Settings never reach loadPlugin (bootstrap handles them) but the loader's mental model is incomplete.
- internal/plugin/manager.go:624-627,928 — m.hosts[TypeLua] vs m.luaHost backward-compatibility duplication with no external compatibility surface to preserve.
- internal/plugin/manager.go:923-945 — TestLoadPlugin is test-only code in production file (no build tag); ships in the binary.

### Inconsistent eventbus error model
- internal/eventbus/types.go:95,99,102,118 + internal/eventbus/codec/registry.go:29 — fmt.Errorf + sentinel pattern at the boundary plugin code crosses, while host wraps results back into oops.With(...).Wrap(err) (cmd/holomush/sub_grpc.go:447). Pick one model.

## Severity

P2 (architecture/maintainability). Each item is small but the collective makes the codebase feel mid-refactor. Several depend on or simplify after ec22.2/ec22.3/ec22.4 land.

title: Plugin-as-caller identity for PluginHostService.QueryStreamHistory against plugin-owned subjects
description: Spec docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-23-plugin-history-authz-design.md §4.4 deferred this. Today busHistoryReaderAdapter.ReplayTail (cmd/holomush/sub_grpc.go:511) passes zero q.Caller, so plugin-owned subjects routed through PluginHistoryRouter return PERMISSION_DENIED to plugin callers. No in-tree caller currently breaks. Before any plugin starts reading plugin-owned subjects via this path, design: (a) what Actor.Kind a plugin caller carries, (b) what capability grants gate cross-plugin reads, (c) whether plugins can read their own subjects without the gate, (d) how a plugin asserts which character (if any) it reads on behalf of.

title: Startup coverage for cmd/holomush/{core,sub_grpc}.go
description: The startup wiring code in cmd/holomush is currently at 0-0.6% patch coverage. E2E tests exercise it structurally but coverage instrumentation isn't observing it. Consider either: (a) extracting the wiring into testable pure functions + thin main shells, or (b) adding an in-process test harness that instruments the full boot sequence. Follow-up from PR #252 Phase B cutover.

title: stream_registry honors explicit ReplayMode instead of silently downgrading
description: CodeRabbit PR #252: internal/grpc/stream_registry.go's AddStreamWithMode and StreamSenderAdapter.Send accept explicit ReplayMode values (BoundedTail, LiveOnly) but since F3 Subscribe always uses cursor-mode replay via SessionStream.SetFilters. Callers requesting bounded-tail or time-based replay silently get cursor-style behaviour. Fix: either (A) reject unsupported modes with an error at send time, or (B) thread the replayMode through Subscribe so SetFilters uses the requested window. Option A is simpler and keeps the existing F8 cutover contract; option B requires extending the SessionStream contract. Ideally do (A) in the next PR and (B) in F9/F10.

title: holomush audit-backfill CLI subcommand
description: Add 'holomush audit-backfill' subcommand to replay JS stream into events_audit for subjects missing from the projection (spec §8 Backfill rebuild). Test skeleton at test/integration/eventbus_e2e/backfill_rebuild_test.go asserts count parity between JS and events_audit. Contract: takes --since/--until flags; idempotent via INSERT ... ON CONFLICT on events_audit.id; reports counts to stdout.

title: eventbus audit drift detector: codec / row tamper checker
description: Build a drift detector that scans events_audit and reports rows with tampered or unknown codec headers, or whose id/subject/type triple is inconsistent with the JS payload (spec §8 invariant: 'Every audit row's codec resolves in registry'). Test skeleton at test/integration/eventbus_e2e/audit_drift_detector_test.go asserts tampered row id is surfaced. Needed to unskip that test.

title: plugin_consumer.go has 0% unit coverage — exercised via E2E only
description: F5 added internal/eventbus/audit/plugin_consumer.go but the dispatch + Start/Stop logic is not unit-tested. End-to-end coverage proves the publish to JS to consumer to plugin RPC to scene_log path works, but unit tests would require an embedded NATS (like projection_test.go) or mock jetstream.Consumer. Add unit tests so the path is independently verifiable.

title: Add Tailscale for remote admin access (optional SSH replacement)
description: Follow-up from PR #246 review. Extend scripts/cloud-init.sh with an optional TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY env var. When set, install tailscaled via the official script, join the tailnet with --auth-key, --hostname, and --ssh (Tailscale SSH mode). Extend .github/workflows/bootstrap-sandbox.yaml with a matching workflow input that passes through to cloud-init. Extend scripts/bootstrap-seed-secrets.sh to optionally prompt for TAILSCALE_AUTH_KEY and save as a GH secret. When Tailscale is enabled, drop the SSH 22/tcp rule from the DO firewall before POSTing (public SSH is no longer needed). Update site/docs/operating/sandbox-operations.md and deployment.md with both admin paths (SSH legacy + Tailscale preferred). Acceptance: bootstrapped droplet joins tailnet, 'tailscale ssh holomush@<host>' works from an authorized device, firewall shows no public 22/tcp when enabled.

title: chore(lint): investigate gocritic+ruleguard loading failure on plugins/ packages
description: Discovered during PR #202 cleanup. After removing the legacy 'plugins/' path exclusion from the lint config (a leftover from a TinyGo WASM plugin design that no longer exists), gocritic's ruleguard checker fails to load gorules/rules.go when scanning files under plugins/. Symptom: 'ruleguard: execution error: used Run() with an empty rule set' on every plugin file. Same config and rules.go work for internal/, cmd/, pkg/, test/. Workaround in PR #202: narrow per-linter exclusion (gocritic only, plugins/ only). The blocked work is adding SceneOpsEventsAppendOnly to gorules/rules.go to enforce scene_ops_events append-only via lint at lint time, repo-wide. Currently enforced by a runtime static check in store_integration_test.go which only runs in task test:int and only scans the plugins/core-scenes/ directory. Investigation paths: relative-path resolution in nested package dirs, absolute path style, known gocritic/ruleguard issues for go-ruleguard/dsl v0.3.23, moving rules.go relative location, minimal rules.go probe to isolate cause.

title: Gateway-side request validation for ConnectRPC endpoints
description:
**Context**

Phase 2 of the scenes rework introduces protovalidate (buf.build/go/protovalidate) as the project's request validation mechanism. The Phase 2 work wires it into the plugin SDK so that every plugin's gRPC server validates inbound requests via a unary interceptor. Plugin handlers can assume their request messages are valid by the time they're invoked.

**The gap**

The gateway sits between web clients and the core server. Web clients talk to the gateway via ConnectRPC; the gateway proxies to core via gRPC; core proxies to plugins via gRPC. Phase 2's plugin-side validation catches invalid requests at the deepest layer (the plugin process), but invalid requests still travel the entire stack — web → gateway → core → plugin — before being rejected.

The gateway has no protovalidate interceptor of its own. Any new ConnectRPC handlers added to the gateway (Phase 9 web client scene endpoints, future cross-cutting RPCs, etc.) currently rely on plugin-side validation as the only enforcement point.

**Why this matters**

1. Performance: invalid requests waste a network round-trip per RPC hop before being rejected
2. Consistency: error message shape varies depending on which layer rejects (gateway vs plugin)
3. Defense in depth: relying solely on the deepest layer means a bug or misconfiguration anywhere upstream can let invalid data through
4. Public surface: the gateway is the only externally-facing entry point — validation belongs there as a baseline, with plugin-side as belt-and-suspenders

**When this becomes urgent**

Phase 9 of the scenes rework adds the web chat view, which means the web client will start calling scene RPCs through the gateway. Without gateway-side validation, the first invalid scene request will travel the full stack. Phase 9 should not be the first time we think about this.

**Constraints / things to consider when picking this up**

- ConnectRPC's interceptor API is different from gRPC's. Both work with the same protovalidate.Validator instance.
- The gateway is a separate binary from the core server and plugin processes — it has its own composition root for any interceptor wiring
- Whatever gets built here should compose with future interceptors (auth, telemetry, rate limiting)

**Out of scope for this bead**

This bead is the *need*. Whoever picks it up should design the actual approach (interceptor placement, validator construction, error mapping, configuration surface) as part of the work.

**Discovered from**

Scenes Phase 2 brainstorm, 2026-04-07. The scope question 'do we wire the gateway-side interceptor in Phase 2?' was answered 'no, defer' specifically so the gateway change gets its own thinking.

title: ABAC resolver: principal.id and resource.id injection (discovered during scenes Phase 1)
description:
Discovered while implementing the read-own-scene ABAC policy in Scenes Phase 1 (holomush-5rh.10).

The ABAC attribute resolver in internal/access/policy/attribute/resolver.go was not injecting the raw entity ID from the colon-prefixed reference. Policies like 'resource.scene.owner == principal.id' could not work because principal.id was always nil unless a provider explicitly set it.

**Fix applied during Phase 1 Task 13:**
The resolver now extracts the ID portion after the colon (e.g., 'char-alice' from 'character:char-alice') and injects it as bags.Subject['id'] / bags.Resource['id'] BEFORE calling resolveEntity. Providers can still override.

**Why this is a follow-up:**
- The fix should be reviewed for any unintended cross-impact on existing ABAC tests
- The test that was modified (resolver_test.go line 483 area) deserves a sanity check
- The behavior change should be documented somewhere (maybe in attribute resolver godoc)
- Consider whether the same id-injection should happen for action references

title: Scenes Phase 10: Notifications + telnet edge cases + polish
description:
Tenth and final phase of the scenes rework. Adds the notification stream for cross-scene awareness, handles telnet edge cases, and polishes the system.

**Spec reference:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-06-scenes-and-rp-design-v2.md (section 3.3, 11)

**Scope:**
